speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I was 10 years old or 9 years old when I came here in 1902.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about him, Wild Davey?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, all I remember was that we were, as kids, we were scared of him because he was kind of a recluse, I guess you'd call him. He was more or less considered him a hermit, sort of. And he had these dogs, which we were afraid of too. So that he was an individual. He was eccentric. People in town here knew of him.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So he was, as I say, a character.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see him mostly going by and on the street? Is that where you'd see him?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, we'd see him down here. He lived right down here on White Avenue, kind of a shack down there. And by the way, too, at that same time, we had a Negro family lived right down in there. And that's the first Negro family that I know of that came to Moscow. And that was here, and they were here before 1902, I'm sure. Caris knows the name of a party, a Negro family, because they were real dark Negroes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he made a living?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did their kids go to school here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't know how many youngsters they had. I suppose she's taping out there. I'd go out and ask her now. I can't think of the name of it. It wasn't a big Negro family at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who'll ask her later? Do you know if they stayed for very long? Do you know if they stayed for very long?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: As far as I know, I don't remember them ever leaving. They may be buried here in the cemetery, as far as I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This Wild Davey, did he have any friends in Moscow?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Mr. Creighton was a particularly good friend he has. And I wouldn't be surprised that he saw that he had clothing to wear. As Caris mentioned, the last time you were here, the hotel gave him food. At that time, the hotel was run by Mr. Gail, whose daughter married Govemex. Govemex was a Gail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any idea why Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Creighton and he were friends?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just took an interest in the man?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Evidently, no.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Creighton was a man that had very few friends. He had special friends that he liked. He was interested in him because he was an immigrant. He was a boy from Scotland, came to the United States. In fact, as I understood it, his folks wanted to make a minister out of him. He didn't want to, and he immigrated to America. I don't know how old he was when he came here. How he happened to come to Moscow, I don't know.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But I worked for Mr. Creighton from 1907 to 1910 while I was going to school. While I know he had special friends, he would send him to a liquor company and get very high -grade wines and liquors. He'd get a whole barrel full of this stuff. He had a little hideaway in the back of the store where he would invite someone that he wanted to talk to secretively. He had that barrel back there. Before Christmas, he would make up the packages of these different people that he would want to give those presents to. I'm sure Wild David probably was maybe one of them too, I don't know. But I know there was a banker in town. He was a special friend.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There were various ones. I delivered some of the packages for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So really, he was the kind of man who didn't hobnob that much with all the businessmen, but kept mostly certain people as...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He had definite friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me, I was thinking about how he was different from Mr. David in their ways of looking at business and at life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How were they different, those two men?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, Mr. David was a community -spirited man. He was interested in education, spiritual life of the town, political, politically. In fact, he could have been governor of the state if he'd wanted to run for it, but he turned it down. I don't know the reason for it, except that it took him away from his business or something.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But Creighton was... He and his wife, he had a wife that was very eccentric.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And she only had a very, very few friends too. So they were that type of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that the two men had a very different philosophy of business?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, they got to be very competitive in years on. And I think that was one of the reasons probably that Mr. Creighton didn't want me to go down to David. Because when I left there, he never spoke to me for several years after I'd been down there. Even his wife wouldn't speak to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me about what that offer was that you said he made you an offer when you came back to say that you were offered a job?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I was getting 25... I think it was $25 a month.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And the opening down there was due to a young fellow that was living at my mother's place. But he and another fellow had a disagreement, and they both quit. So they needed somebody quick. So this fellow told me, he says, if you want the job, go down there and see Mr. David. And they're particularly interested in someone that can understand the Scandinavian people because this county was probably 60 % Scandinavian. So I went down there and I just got the job that quick. So I went back and told Creighton about it. And at first he just kind of blew up and wouldn't say anything. And I gave him plenty of time before I went down to David's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So eventually he came around and he says, will you stay if I boost your salary?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he boosted it up to, I think it was $35 or $40 a month. And I had to tell him that I said my offer down there was better than that. And that I had already accepted the job so I couldn't refuse him. I was of that nature. At the same time, I had an offer from R .S. Shurphy who ran the bookstore. And he offered me the same amount, but I decided to go to Creighton's, or to David's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Creighton ever mention to you about his getting shot by Steffen back a few years before that?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Not that I know of, but I knew of the incident. Because we came here the spring after all this happened. So I knew that Creighton was walking down the street and Steffen took a shot at him. And he was on Steffen's list. Creighton and Watkins, Dr. Watkins, and it seems to me the sheriff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think Jollie was on his list and also held if I'm mistaken.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, August held was on the list too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you hear, what was the talk when you came about what had happened? Did they have any motivation for Steffen's going after Watkins? Any reason, thought?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I really don't know. He was a little off -balanced, but he did have a grudge against Doc Watkins, all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for some reason or other, it was because of some medical, or what? Did they think that from what you heard that Steffen had had a history of being unbalanced, or was it something that just showed itself before the killing?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, evidently he showed some peculiarity because when he went on this rampage, he went on horseback.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And as soon as it was all over, why he should go back to his mother's home, or he's living with his mother?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't know. He thought he'd hit out someplace, but he didn't. And then of course, then when they surrounded the place and tried to get him to surrender, and she finally came out of the house, and they had shot holes into the house. We saw holes in the house years afterwards, after he was shot. So we don't know whether he shot himself or he was shot by a bullet through the house, but he was killed right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any idea how the mother took it?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I suppose like any mother, she wanted to save her son, didn't want to see him killed, but took a lot of nerve for her to come out the door and try to get him to quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was she doing? Was she telling him he was dead, or she telling him?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, as far as I know, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think she came out to say, just tell him to stop shooting before he died?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I suppose coming out of the house, she thought maybe that would stop the shooting. Well, there were a lot of men out there with guns, but they got from the hardware store and went out there and the whole sheriff set up. They were in the trenches in the Paradise Creek out there for protection.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the men who had been in the posse and some of those guys ever talk about it in the later years?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, the papers were full of it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: My father was out here when it happened, and we were still back in Wisconsin. We didn't come out here until about the first of March. And of course he had papers that, oh, the big headlines. I imagine there's papers someplace at the university or someplace that show what happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They have the Lewiston paper, but not the Moscow one. And the account, they have two accounts. I was just looking at this the other day. The one that came out right after he was shot the same day was quite different than the one that came out the next day. They looked it over and thought about it, and really a number of the details were different, whether he had killed himself or been killed, what had happened after he shot Watkins and some of the encounters that he had in Moscow, this sort of thing. The way you heard it was that the mother probably came out trying to get them to stop shooting while he was still defending himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I would take it that was the reason she came out, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. See, they have that two ways too. She came out before or she came out after he was dead, one way or the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did the men talk about it in later years, the men who had been in the posse?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, like any incident of that kind, it never dies down. Sometimes it gets magnified as to what all happened.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I remember seeing some headline of what happened, all right, but I kind of forgot a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did anybody want to take credit on what Clyde suggested to me that some of these guys had some doubts in later years, some of the guys that had been in the posse? Did you ever hear that, that they had doubts? Any guys say that they were sorry? Maybe that they handled it that way?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, I don't remember seeing or reading anything about that. Oh, I was thinking in the talk, I don't think it would be something in print.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, I don't remember anything about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about your mother's boarding house, about how that worked and what it was like.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, after my father passed away in 1907, I think it was, he left her insurance, and she was advised to use that insurance to buy a home. She'd have a place to live because we were renting at that time. We had sold the home that we built in 1902, so that was for sale and we bought it back.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And in order to, there were four of us youngsters, and I was the oldest at the time. In order to keep the family together, she decided to run a boarding house for students, which she did, and in order to do it, we had three bedrooms upstairs and one down, and a parlor, and she turned the parlor into a bedroom. So that gave her four rooms for students. Well, she had about six to eight students they slept to in her room, and she was a wonderful cook, and she boarded and roomed them for $20 a month, and she set a very, very good table. They all liked her food. Well, that left it pretty tough for us to, for sleeping quarters, so a brother and I, we slept in the dining room in a pull -down bed, which we had to put up every morning so that they'd have room enough to eat around a big dining room table.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then, as far as toilet facilities, of course we had a metal house. That was the only thing we had there. The old outhouse with a crescent over the door, and a series of robot catalogs, which the building had to be moved about every year or so. We had no water supply. We had to drill our own wells, so we had a pump, which we had to carry water into the kitchen for drinking and cooking and all that. We had no bathroom, and of all things, having students, younger fellows, they had to take a bath in the kitchen in a tub, a metal tub. The mother would have on the back of the stove a boiler boiling hot water, and they'd just take water out of there and put it in the tub, and that's where they took their bath. Well, we had an electrical engineering student stay there from Wallace. The mother wouldn't let him use very big light bulbs because electricity was rather expensive.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He worked out a lot of where he could put a wire ahead of the meter and bring it into his room, which he did. As far as I know, that operated for years that way. We didn't know anything about it, but we found out afterwards.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: These fellows were all around 20, 21, 22, along in there. One was a mining engineer, one was an electrical engineer, two of them were civil engineers. It's remarkable we kept top of those fellows after they graduated and where they went, and every one of them made good.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I remember Harry Driscoll for one, but she's an old -time family that he lived there.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He operated a bean business out of Troy and made a metamunning on that. Another fellow was from Genesee, Oley Pauls. He wound up in the federal government with road construction and bridges and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, anyway, they all...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did these fellows, most of them stayed for several years in the boarding house? Did they stay for more than one year?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: She operated that for about six or seven years. In fact, I was working at Creighton's at the time when she started it. She carried it on even for several years after I went down to David's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the fellows date? Did they go out on dates in those days, these students? What were you about? The university women who were at the university at the time?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that who they...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah. Yes, they were. I can tell you one incident, but it wasn't due to be recorded. One of the fellows stayed there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe you could tell it without using his name.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I'll take it from me. It's kind of an immoral thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where the wood came from that you heated with?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, we had a wood stove, both in the living room and dining room. A wood stove, wood and coal rains as she cooked on. We had what they called the people that lived out in the mountains. They called them wood rats. During the wintertime, they would cut four foot length timber and sell it then, the next summer. For the following winter's fuel. As I remember, my father used to buy eight cords of wood at a time. We had a wood shed, a pilot outside the wood shed. There was a fellow named Stanley who... Two of the sons who were still living in Moscow, Hubert Stanley and Rick Stanley. He had a saw machine put on the back of a truck.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He would cut these into 16 or 18 foot lengths. 16 foot lengths because it was four foot cordwood.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then both my sisters and brother, we piled the wood in the wood shed. Then later on, we mixed coal along with the wood to give more heat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The general cost of things was very low then.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: As I remember, you would buy a cord of wood for around four and a half to six dollars a cord in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your mother could actually make a living by the rates that she was charging these fellas.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't know how she did it, but she was interested in my two sisters taking music. She bought a piano on a monthly payment basis and paid that out and my two sisters learned to play the piano. As far as my brother and myself were concerned, at that time there was a man in the club organized in Moscow. A man from Spokane came down and gave lessons.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The instructor was a guitar player. Then there was a man named Childers who run an ice cream parlor, played the guitar. Then I played the guitar, so there were three of us and about 25 mandolins. He furnished him a mandolin for nothing and gave him instructions. I think the course for six lessons was twelve dollars. When he finished the course, we gave a concert in the Methodist Church for the city.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People could play after six lessons then.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Pretty good. After that, there were four of us formed a little mandolin club of our own and played at churches and lodges and different organizations around town. That group was, and I went down yesterday to see the Egan girl, who her cousin was one of the group. It was Signe Egan, Jenny Peterson, who was Klaus Peterson's daughter, and my sister Myrtle and myself. We played for a good many years after that. Really, our final playing was up at the Lutheran Church, which I was a member of. All our pieces were waltzes and two steps and things like that. We didn't play any religious hymns or anything like that. We had enough fundamentalists in the church that objected to having that kind of music in the church. We never played after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You weren't playing at a church service just in the church, right?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It was an evening service of some kind. It wasn't a church service, but it was in the church. I didn't think that was the place for that kind of music. That was the days when we had a lot of people in Moscow that were against playing cards and dancing, both. A lot of them were Methodists. I think the Presbyterians were a little more lenient. Lutherans were against it, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've been told that it was really those people who felt strongly that way that did bring about prohibition coming to Moscow when it came. That's right.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That's right.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We had prohibition pretty early.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Seems to me it was before World War I. I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about a lot of temperance lectures? It was put on the ballot or what happened?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It was on the ballot, of course. The WCTU were the instigators of the whole thing. They passed around a lot of propaganda. During prohibition, the little towns across the line, Union Town and Colton, most places, were still open. Washington didn't have prohibition. Of course, it was very easy to get liquor just going over there. It got to the point where the sheriffs would set up a posse out here on the South Main Street bridge and check cars that they thought were coming over from Union Town with liquor.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then when it got more severe, then the bootleggers came in here and they went around and took individual orders, never delivered right on the spot. They took orders and then it was delivered later. I remember a lot of incidents like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When it got more severe, you mean when it was harder to get? You just couldn't go across to Washington and get it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I tried.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it openly violated? It was done very quietly, but still it was pretty broadly disregarded?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, that was it. They knew that they were watching the liquor coming in.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But it got so common that they didn't enforce it very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard it said that it brought about more drinking because it was illegal. More people got interested in it. It was forbidden.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That's why prohibition is overturned, just for that reason, that there was more drinking. A lot of people were getting liquor from stills out in the mountains and people were operating stills, which were... Well, there were cases where people went blind drinking some of the liquor. It wasn't properly distilled.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So that's what defeated prohibition when Roosevelt came in. I've forgotten what year he overturned that, but it must have been after the 30s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was the early 30s. I wanted to ask you a little bit more about Mr. David, but first I was going to ask you about that book that you mentioned to me that was an influence on you in your early years.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, Mr. David, Sr., Frank David took quite an interest in me. I was interested in my job. I did a lot of things that I wondered if I wouldn't do. He appreciated me doing more than I was expected.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I showed quite an interest in selling. He realized that because I would tell him when I made a good sale and things like that. He had a book, really a book on psychology, because he said selling is all psychology, anyway. In this book, he took it rather seriously, too, himself. He said, you know, you can tell a customer by his handshake, whether it's a limp shake or a real hearty shake. I said, you can judge also a customer by the coarseness of his hair, things of that type.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did coarse hair mean?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, and I found this particularly true with a lot of customers. A person with coarse black hair normally bought coarse materials and clothing, like tweeds and things like that. It wasn't a thing for them to buy because it accentuated their hair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they were attracted to it because it was like their hair?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Evidently.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the handshake? What was a limp handshake or a strong handshake?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: A very weak person.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: A strong person would be harder to wait on than the other. The weaker were more, easier, influenced into what you were trying to sell them. And even their walk and things like that. This was all those things that are in the selling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was a book about selling or a book about psychology or both?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It was Psychology of Selling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find the book, that book, useful?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh yes. I read that thing through and through, time and time.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And then up at the university, they gave a night course in the business school and the psychology department. And I took that in for about three months, I guess.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We had a fellow come through here. But most of the business people on Main Street attended it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the fellow that nationally advertised? Was that Dale Carnegie?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, Dale Carnegie. That's it. And they sent a man in here and he set up a week's course on selling. And I think that was brought about by the business school at the university, which was very helpful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you seek advice from Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: David very often about all the technique and the skills involved in the work?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find him helpful in the...

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Very much so because he was a salesman himself. In fact, he was a good salesman. And he would come around and talk to me about selling. And naturally I would listen to him and probably ask questions from him about the technique of selling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much opportunity to watch him working in action?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, a senior, Mr. David, at the time I went in there, was more of a forewalker. He did very little selling, but he directed customers to people he wanted them to wait on him.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And he was a great storyteller.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He would talk to the customers and he'd collaborate with them. With women that were good customers, he'd always go back and get a sack of candy and give it to them. When they paid their bill at the end of the year, the farmers around here and a lot of people had yearly accounts, he'd always bring them to me, to the men, and he says, I want you to fix John up with a good hat. He just paid his bill. So I gave away many a hat to people. At that time you could buy a Mallory hat made out of rabbits for three dollars. That was the price I remember now. A Stetson hat was five dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say he directed people to people we wanted to wait on, he had in mind, do you think, who would match his customers with his salesman?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think so.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I'm sure he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like a real good business sense to me.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he was. I consider him one of the highlights in making my career.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He had eventually three sons in the store, but when I went in there, there were two sons. One operated the shoe department and the other the clothing department. It was only about three years or four. The son that was running the clothing department, I was home of David, he wanted to take charge of the women's reticure in the draggers department. He had that up. He turned the department then over to me, which I was about 19 or 20 at that time. But he would consult with me several years after I took it over. But after 19, I would say after 1921, he turned the whole thing over to me and I operated it until the time I quit in 1958.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I'll have to admit I was rather successful operating it because in all the years that I was in the store, the men's department had the biggest volume and the most profitable during those years. I had the figures down in the basement. In the year I went in until the time I quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I could ask you to what do you attribute that success, but I don't think I will because you probably would be too modest to want to tell me.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, never so.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I gave them plenty. Plenty of my time. A 10 hour day didn't mean anything to me. In those days we went to work on 7 o 'clock and quit at 6. And we on Saturdays and holidays, they're not holidays, but when there was a fair or a chautauqua or any special event in town, we'd keep open until probably 10 o 'clock at night. So hours didn't mean anything to me and of course I was interested in seeing that the department improved all the time. So we were continually rearranging the department town. Senior Mr. David went right along with me. So you go ahead and do it. It remained a lot of new innovations.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, take the men's hats. We had to sell hats out of a hat box. A hat box held three hats. So you had a tremendous lot of hat boxes up there. And when you wanted to sell a hat, well you had to take a box down, get the hat off, and if you showed him more than one style, you probably had four or five boxes out there. It made a kind of a clutter. So I proposed that he let me put in a shelving and put these hats out of the boxes. Well, he went a little further than that. He even says, well, we'll put some glass over it. So that was one thing. Then a dressing room. The only way a man could try on a pair of trousers was to go up to the third floor. Well, that meant going up three sets of stairs to get up there, and it took you away from the department. Well, you were doing all that because you had to measure the trousers and all that. So I talked to him to let me put in a dressing room downstairs, and the only place that we had, and we'd only allow a space about four by four for a man to go in and put on a pair of trousers. Well, that helped an awful lot, and we used that for a good many years. Then our stock room was up on the third floor against the south wall, where we kept surplus of underwear and overalls and shirts and luggage and all that. So I proposed that he let us put in a balcony because the ceilings were about 18 feet tall. There was a ledge all around the side walls, so he allowed me to go ahead and put that in. We moved down a lot of, especially the soft guns, downstairs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did the balcony make that possible, putting in the balcony? Putting in the balcony, what was the reason to put it in?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So we could take some of the reserves stock upstairs and put it on the shelf. We wouldn't have to run up there every time we wanted something we didn't have down on the floor. We had just a ladder to get up there. It wasn't a stairway, it was just a ladder that went up there. Well, then when I first went in the store, a lot of the men's suits were folded with the pants and the vest put inside and put on the counter. You had one here and one here that folded over into this pile, and you had a stack about that high. You had it in sizes and different stacks, and if you wanted a certain pattern, you had to pull it out of the stack, which was a terrible nuisance, and it messed things up awfully. So I got them to get the plumber to make some racks out of the water pipe, and we hung the suits from then on racks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of these ideas just came from your own observations of what the problems were.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, anytime that I took a vacation or made a trip anyplace, my main objective on the trip was to go into other stores and see how they arranged their merchandise and what makes the handles. In fact, I even shop window displays because each department had to trim their own windows. So I would observe their windows, and I took notes of what they had on their sign cards and their windows and all that in order to pass that on out of the store.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And we did put in some pretty good windows because we had one line of clothing, Style Plus, which sold for $17, and they were giving a prize for the best window. Well, I did not realize that there were some big stores that had some elaborate windows, but I went in and decorated a window with all the material I sent, and I had the photographer take a picture of it and send it in. Well, I did not get a money prize, but I did get an honorable mention on it. So that was something. And then remember in the early merchandising business, you did not have janitors in the store. You swept the floor every night before you left or early in the morning when you came down. You shoveled the sidewalks off, snow in the wintertime, washed the windows, all that. There was no special one. We all did it. We all pitched in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many salesmen were there in your department besides yourself?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There were three of us when I first went in there. And then we increased it to four. And then during World War I, because we had a tremendous lot of soldiers up here in all the dormitories and the fraternity houses and so on. And then when we started hiring students. And there have been times when I have had as many as four students in the department.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds to me that probably you are more interested in advancing the business than you were in advancing yourself in a way or just making money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Am I correct in feeling that way?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, as I think of it now, I was so interested in my job and there was so much to learn and do that time didn't mean a thing. I just spent hours and hours, even over time, because I was interested in it. And I guess you would say I was more interested in the business than I was the money that I made. I was just looking the other day.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: 2, and 3, I got $125 a month. And then I got up to $150 a month before the Depression started in 30. And then the Depression hit us so hard a year after it started nationally that they just got us together and said, This is about the only thing we can do. We are going to have to reduce salaries. And they reduced my salary to $125 and others accordingly.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But along about $36, $35, I guess, things started to pick up. And of course, I want to try it on up. $175 a month. We had a tremendous increase in business after World War II. In fact, I had one of the biggest years I ever had right after World War II. I think it was in 1948 or 1949 in the department.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this year the year that everyone took a salary cut? Was that 1930 that year?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I would judge it, presumably, in 1931 or 1902, because it was after Roosevelt got in.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It hit merchants in the bigger communities, much worse than it hurt us to start out with. The Depression didn't get here until a year after the others were involved.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about Mr. David's possible running for governor that you mentioned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what that situation was that he could have run if he wanted to?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he was offered it. I know that.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The only reason I can see why he didn't take it is that he didn't want to be involved in the campaign for it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: His business meant a lot to him. It would take him away from the business. It's proven that in Governor McConnell. When he took governorship, the business went down. Eventually, he had to close out when he got back from his four years as governor. Do you know what role Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: David took in politics?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He and his sons both were tremendously interested in the university.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: At the time, Pocatello was interested in getting the university down there away from us. Every legislature had to fight that. Mr. David was very instrumental in the back room politics and contacting the legislature by phone from Moscow.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was very political -minded and he was very aggressive in it. I guess today you would call him a semi -liberal because he was looking out for the interest in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would he be liberal?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was for things that a lot of people were against.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think Moscow was divided?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were a lot of people in Moscow who didn't really care about having the university here.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't think that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the kinds of things?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The outside interests were trying to get it away from us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would some of the things be that he was for and that other people wouldn't be for?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, this being an agricultural community, that was the main resource here for business because Moscow was kind of a trading point of this whole area.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: All his old cronies were interested in the university and that influenced him, I think some too. The university enrollment, when they started out, was very, very small. Even when I went into the store in 1910, I don't think the enrollment was over 650 to 700 students and it was building up.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Of course, he had sons that he was interested in, education. Every one of them went through college.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Three of them, three sons and a daughter, graduated from the university from 1902 to about 1909. Howard David, the youngest, I think, graduated from 1909.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That was an incentive, of course, because he was interested in education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your understanding about how the university got here in the first place?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, of course, the old story is that we had the opportunity, or boys who had the opportunity of the penitentiary or the university and they picked the penitentiary. That's the old story. But I think it was through the efforts of some of our early politicians in the late 80s. I can't recall the names. McConnell was one, of course. And Willis Sweet is one. Yes. We had a push here to put that thing over where the other fellows didn't.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It was through the people that we had here, living here at the time, Judge Forney, for instance, is another. He was the first appointed president.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He kept the ball rolling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you subscribe to the idea that it was largely given to us to keep North Idaho as a part of the state so that they wouldn't go and join Washington?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, that was brought up by, I don't know whether Sweet was one of them, but I'm sure the early educators or early politicians used that as a leverage on Southern Idaho. What they wanted to do was take in Spokane, let Spokane be the capital of the northern eastern Washington, northern Idaho, and western Montana. And the new state was to have been Lincoln.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think it was brought up more than once, as a threat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it probably had quite a bit of support up north here, that idea.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You bet they did.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, yes, there were a lot of people who were in favor of that because it was a fight with the South all the time. And it's a funny thing. When Idaho was established, Northern Idaho was a progressive end of the state. Southern Idaho was nothing but sagebrush, and farming got started a little later, of course. And it got to be, in a Boise fell, it got to be quite an agricultural and fruit area.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Now, I think it was due to the men, the politicians that we had here that got the unversed here, the penitentiary end of it, but I don't think it was in the picture at all.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There was another book that I was going to ask you about, and that is the book that your mother gave you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And is the book about pushing to the front?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, it is how people became successful in their field.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, there are a lot of national figures in there, of course, that they use as examples.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think you'll find an article there on Benjamin Franklin. I wouldn't be surprised, even Napoleon's in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It says, wanted a man, possibilities and spare moments. Choosing a vocation, the triumphs of enthusiasm, personality as a success asset. Do it to a finish.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The whole idea of the book was to put yourself into whatever you intended. Push yourself right along.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Don't let failure stop you from pursuing what you're interested in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this book very important to you when you first had it?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, yeah. I read it through and through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Give you more confidence that a young man could make good in.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Sure did. When you could see what others did, you were able to go up to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I see Cornelius Vanderbilt in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We were through his mansion two years ago. The most beautiful mansion I've ever seen. A lot of marvel.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There was a place for entertainment. Had a big ballroom. Lots of bedrooms.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: A lot of people were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this book aimed at business or was it aimed at business, government and any career?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Any career. It wasn't anything in particular. It was to instill in you to push yourself into whatever you attempted to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the circuses coming to Moscow when you were a boy?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, yes. We had a circus, I would say, every year. I don't know that we ever had a Ringling Brothers circus here, but we had Cels Floto.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There was another one that used to come.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: They usually sat up in the old fairgrounds down where Rosauers are. We have had circuses down at Ghormley Field. We've had some where they had to get out on the outskirts of town to perform. The city always got a little revenue off of circuses for use of ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As a boy, did you want to go to those when you could?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Not only that, but I carried water to the elephants. I got many a pass into a circus. We'd always go down there early in the morning. We'd have to carry boards for the seats, things of that type. Many a time I carried water to the animals.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You returned for a pass?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, you didn't get paid for it. You just got a pass. You didn't have to work for long to get a pass.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Another place where they had circuses was down there where Mark IV was a circus ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they very large? The circuses?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Three rings.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Once in a while a small water circus would come that would be mostly a dog hill or something like that. Most of the circuses are three ringed circuses, acrobats and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lions? Did they have lions?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, lions. I have a set of lions, tigers, horses, monkeys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about these chautauquas? Do you remember them? The chautauquas?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes. Very much so. They'd come for a week and sell season tickets before you had to buy a season ticket. I remember there were six dollars for a season ticket for a week. They set up in a tent just north of City Park. That was all open ground there then. Their program was both afternoons and evenings.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We had some nationally known figures at these chautauquas because chautauquas had some pretty fine programs. I'll never forget one program. Either a congressman or a senator was on the program. It was right before World War I.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There is no war coming up. These countries can't afford a war. Germany is broke. France hasn't got money enough to carry on a war. I'll be no war. He had an odd time of week. We had a war started. I'll never forget that.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: They had some wonderful programs and wonderful entertainment. That was both musical and speech and lectures and whatnot. I was playing the cornet at an orchestra at that time. Egan's Hall was built somewhere around nineteen four or five. That's where the shopping center is on Third Street. He had a photographer gallery right on the corner. He built this big wooden building with a stage. It was a roller rink. People in town here roller skated for several years.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We had dances there. During the chautauqua, our orchestra played for ten cent dances. That was the common thing in those days. You didn't go to a dance and pay a couple of dollars for the evening. It was a ten cent dance. After every dance, we felt like we were on the tickets. That was very profitable for us because there were some of those evenings that the place was so full that we made around twelve, fifteen dollars apiece. We were playing for those dances, which was a lot of money in those days.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then after that, the National Guard was formed here in Moscow. I was a member of it. We used the stage for the lockers to keep our guns in and equipment. I remember we used to drill up and down Main Street and Third Street. The only real activity I had in the National Guard was when the IWW uprisings were on. They were mostly lockers. They set up what they called a bull pen down in Ghormley Park. The fence around it regarded that fence so that they didn't break out. The thing was finally settled.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think I served two years in the National Guard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about how long they kept the bull pen?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It wasn't very long, probably a week. That was settled.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Most of them were lockers from up in the camps that were causing the difficulty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about the forming of the First Country Club. How that came about here.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think I gave you a lot of that. It was only just us talking. We didn't put it down on tape. I thought the story of that would be good. The beginning of golf in Moscow started with Albert Moody and myself down on the old fairground, which is now Ghormley Park. My brother did a lot of the labor on it. We put in six golf holes, sand greens,

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: and had them oiled and so on. It was pretty good. There were just enough people in Moscow that had played golf in other places before they came to Moscow. Around that nucleus, we got the thing going at a dollar per memory to put those greens in. We had no membership fee particularly. That was about it, one dollar for playing for the season.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any troubles laying out that golf course there?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, because we had one hole that was next to the Paradise Crick.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: A lot of golf balls were lost in the Crick, of course, until they got out of a floater golf ball. Whenever we on a second shot were afraid that it was going into the Crick, we always used a floater. That saved it. You did not get the distance on that kind of a ball that you did out of a regular golf ball. Well, from that, it progressed into wanting a nine -hole golf course. That was before World War I. We went out on the Gov mix place north of town where the KRPL station is now and laid out nine holes. They were sand greens, too.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We only operated that. At that time, that is when we organized the Moscow Country Club, which took in both golf and tennis. The tennis courts were up on the corner of Hayes and B Street. I think we had three or four tennis courts. To become a member of both golf and tennis, you paid $15. If you only played one, you paid $10. I have a list of the names of all the original members of that course. Well, the war came along, and we were criticized so in developing a golf course, because it was not completed until yet. Of us putting in a golf course when they needed wheat so bad during that time. We even had our grass seed bought, all stacked up ready to plant the thing into greens and so on, and the fairway. Well, they got together, and I decided maybe they should give it up until after the war, which they did, but we still held a group together. Then, I had forgotten just what year it was, but I think it must have been around 21 or 22 when they bought this piece of ground for the golf courses now.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I hired Mr. James, who was a golf expert in laying out greens. He came here and laid out the course. In order to develop that, we had to get a membership. We had over 100 memberships at $125 a piece, and that started the thing off. I think the green fees, or not the green fees, but even though you were a charter member, later on, your green fees were $60 a year. Well, that dropped a lot of the membership, and our membership went down. We hated to see the course go to the boards, and the Elks Temple at the time had slot machines, and they were making money hand over fist on their slot machines.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We proposed to them, because a lot of our members were Elks, that we would give them, turn it over to them, if they wanted to operate it. They took us up, and it has been the Elks Golf Course ever since. The sad part of it, those of us that paid $125 as charter members of the course, never got any membership. We had to buy a new membership, just the same as we never owned it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you still paying off the land? Is that why it became impossible to hold on to the course yourselves?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Let's see now, it was 40 acres, and I don't think the land at that time cost over $100 an acre, so the 40 acres didn't cost over $4 ,000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the problem was the upkeep was too much for you, too expensive.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, that was part of it. The factory just couldn't, there wasn't money enough taken in to operate it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you tell me that there was, in the early first days of golf here, there was a lot of people, especially businessmen, who just didn't think anything of the sport, didn't want to try it?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well that was the original course down at the fairgrounds. In fact, I think I mentioned to you that it took us quite some time to get some of the older falls interested in it. And Mr. David Sr., who was up in years at that time, wouldn't be seen carrying a golf club out of the store or a golf bag. So I used to play with him, occasionally, and we would go out the back door of the store and put his golf clubs in the car and drive down there and play around and come back and bring them in the same way. So there were several of them thought it was kind of a sissy game, sickening up a ball and driving it and chasing it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But it got so that they got tremendously, well they had to get tremendously interested in it to what I put in a nine hole course out north of town. And it developed pretty fast after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard to come by golf clubs in the first days of golf around here?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Now just as soon as we organized that first golf course, I put in an athletic department in the store. And I was the only one in town that had golf clubs to start out with. And in those days there were no steel shaft golf clubs. They were all wooden shafts, which were the kind they used in Scotland where golf originated. And a golf club in those days, I even have a listing of the clubs that I sold of various memories, would run two and a half a piece. A driver, a mauche, would cost three dollars. And golf balls, I remember, were from 25 cents to 50 cents.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And I sold a lot of golf clubs in there. And from that way, we got interested in selling equipment to the university for their football and basketball and tennis and all that. And for a good many years, we were the suppliers for the university. Basketball and football came on their equipment. Uniforms, footballs, basketballs and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did men carry bags for the clubs in those days?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And then of course when we started this course out here, some of those older fellows wanted somebody else to carry their bag. And that's when a lot of kids in town here went out there and acted as patties. And on my adopted son, I took him out, this was in the late 30s, I took him out to carry my bag for me and let him learn a little something about golf. Well, it didn't last very long because there were so many golf balls lost in the rough. But these kids were out there hunting these golf balls because you could turn them into the clubhouse if the ball was in good shape for 25 cents apiece. Well, I don't know how many dozens of golf balls that he picked up, but I lost my caddy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I was going to ask you also along with talking about clubs, about the three clubs here in town, the major ones. And I know the main thing I was interested in was how they sort of came about. Now, the rotary was here first and the Kiwanis came later. And so the rotary was sort of looked at as the old man's club and then the Kiwanis was the young man's club.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, and what about the coming of the lions and what you were telling me about the feeling that the town couldn't support them all?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, it seemed that both the rotary and Kiwanis were a little lax in taking on more members into their club. And evidently some national officer of lions came into Moscow and surveyed the community and thought the lions of the club should be organized here. So they got hold of a few of the younger fellows. They were doctors and bankers. They were working in banks and so on. And they had a meeting and decided to form a lions club. I think they had to have at least 25 men in order to form a club. Well, I heard about them organizing it and I heard about it a little too late. When I went to the president of the rotary club and I proposed to him, I said, let's go see some of these fellows that are on the list for the lions club. And you take half of them and the Kiwanis take half of them and we'll eliminate the club and the town. Because we didn't think three clubs, the town was big enough for three clubs. But it was too late. They had gone too far and they went ahead and organized. Well, I was there at the organization meeting by the way from Kiwanis as a representative of Kiwanis the night they initiated their officers. And they called on me to say a few words. Of course, I gave them all the praise in the world because there was no use doing anything else.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So, it wasn't many years that their main project was to see how lions specialize on blind people, getting white canes and look after them. It wasn't long. They had several cases of that here. And they got a lot of publicity. Being a young club, they were just looking for community projects galore. And I don't know how it is now, but I would say for years.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And I guess they're still going to get there. There's more community service than either the Rotary or the Kiwanis Club.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And it's a good strong club tonight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think was the big main difference between the Rotary and Kiwanis, those two clubs, the first clubs?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, naturally, Rotary being the first service club organized, they were a little jealous of their club. And I suppose they felt the same way when Kiwanis organized that they didn't want Kiwanis in Moscow. But they were a very active club when they started, too. And they performed a lot of community service.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you in on the organization of Kiwanis?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I was within six months of it, yes. I'm not a charter member. I was opposed for membership because they take two of a business, Rotary take one. And I was approached on it, and I told them, I said, yes, I would be glad to belong, but the store would have to take care of the initiation fee and so on. Well, they saw the store, Homer David, particularly, and he was a Rotarian. And him being a Rotarian and his brother, two brothers with a Chamber of Cramers, they didn't think it was advisable. But in six months, they changed their tour room. I've been a member of the club since 1926. There was plenty of room then for a second club in town at that time. When I was president of the club in 1940, we had a membership of 50, and we kept building it up. We had 65, and then when I was Lieutenant Governor in 1952, I think it was, we got information from the National Headquarters, or the district here, that every club should increase their membership according to the population. And I went and built the club up to 85 members in that year. And of course, we took a lot of members in there that were enthusiastic to begin with, but a lot of them dropped out later on. We had some members that shouldn't have killed on ends at all. All service clubs had that trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I want to think we should hit some more highlights of scouting, too. Now, we did talk about scouting some last time. Let me see what the car says.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Turn on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You have down here on scouting.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, one of the camps that we used out in the mountains before we built the one we finally used was Camp Conjagty, which was Eldridge's cottage home out in the mountains. And he allowed us to use it for overnight hikes and trips out there. And I remember one overnight hike we had out there. I don't know whether I told that to you before, but I had about 24 boys out there sleeping in their blankets. This was before the days of sleeping bags. And one of the boys started to break out and redetermine it. It must be measles. So there was a farm across the valley up there, and we went to there and called up his father and told him, he said, you've got a sick boy out here. You better come out and get him. So he came out and got him, and that's what he had was measles. The thing about it, there was a single boy who caught the measles from that contact out there, which we were glad about. You moved quickly, Alan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This camp in Jogadiy, you slept inside the cottage?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We slept both in and out. The cottage isn't very big, so it didn't hold for any boys. So we slept on the ground, and I think the patrol leaders and the scout officers slept in the cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Dean Eldridge's?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was used frequently by those guys?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, that was for users a lot, because it was close. And of course, scouts, the early scouts loved hiking, and that was a big activity in scouting here, the early scouting. We did a lot of hiking. And in order to pass their first -class test, they had to take a 14 -mile hike. And out to Camp Jogadiy, it was seven miles out there, so they could go out there and come back. And on the hike, of course, they had to do a good turn and observe the landscape and animals and wildlife and all that on this hike, and then tell what they brought with them to cook, or the open campfire and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I want to ask you a little bit about that, since I've been looking at some of those themes, some of these reports that the scouts wrote after the hike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing I'm curious about is, was there a usual route that the boys hiked from here to there on, or were there a lot of different ones?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, there were different ones.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There were good many of them took that, but there were hikes all over. Some of them took them up to Paradise Mountain, Tomer's Butte. I think there's one hike that I gave you there, that they went out south of town and out towards the old Pullman Road and back in, which was longer than a 14 -mile hike. So, no, there was no particular place they had to go to. The main thing, they had to perform a good turn. They had to be 14 miles, and it was an observation test as much as anything. They practiced pacing on it, which was valuable to them in map making.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they stick to the roads mostly?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd go cross -country?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, they'd cross -country. In fact, I was just reading one the other day where they went through a fellow's wheat field, and it was money, and how they had to clean their shoes off after they got out on the highway or the road. We had one hike. We started right here at the corner of the cemetery, County Road, a beeline hike. That was quite an event. We spotted a tree up here that was in the open up here on Paradise Mountain. We were supposed to make a beeline for that. It was called a beeline hike.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I was on that hike, and I had an assistant scout master with me on that hike. The first obstacle we run into was this South Palouse Creek down here. The water was high.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We couldn't go around, and we'd come back over here and then come back over. We had to go cross that creek. The assistant scout master, I always remember, his name was Cleek. He says, I'll get these boys across. He went out and waited the creek to see how deep it was. Well, it came up to about here on him. He took every one of those kids—I've forgotten how many—he was about fifteen. He took every one of those kids, taking that across that creek. We made our beeline hike up there, and we had our cooking along with us, and we had our meal up there. We waste -beaked that water. Yeah, it was cold. I don't know how he took it. He even took me across. He was a big man. He was a pretty husky man, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were the kids know the routes that they took on these fourteen -mile hikes? Have they been over the routes before?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to have them run over and just— The trails were fairly well set in most places that kids would take, and they would know how to get a shortcut from one place to another.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No one got lost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People didn't get lost. The kids didn't get lost then. I was going to ask you about that, whether there were times when the scouts did get lost.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I only had one case, and that was when Moscow Mountain was on fire, had a forest fire up there, and the whole community went out there to dig trenches and stop it. They needed somebody to patrol these trenches after they were dug, because the roots of the tree would go underneath and start a fire on the other side of the trench. So I offered my troop to go out there and patrol. I'd forgotten how many boys we had, but we used camp con -joc -ity as our headquarters for eating meals. I think there was some organization that brought out food for us. Well, we patrolled those trenches, and it was a good thing because we had any number of cases where the roots had gone across the trench and we were able to put them out. But this one boy named Harold Waters strayed off. There were trails like this all over the top there, and it was what they called a round top. All that round top, there were trails led in all directions. He ventured up to that round top, and he took the wrong trail back to camp con -joc -ity. He was lost there for several hours. He hadn't showed up, so we set out a search crew. We kept howering and howering and so on. He'd gotten away, caught a ways, because he took the wrong trail. But he finally heard us, and we stayed where we were and kept howering and he found us. That's the only time I ever had anyone lost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One feeling that I get from reading these things, which I think is so interesting, is this feeling that the country was so much more country in those days than it is today. The scouts would get out there just walking in and they'd be seeing wildlife, a lot of it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It just sounds like there were a lot more trails around than we'd have any traces of these days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, remember this was in 1920, the 20s, 20s and 30s. A lot of towns people used to picnic out in the mountains. That was one of the favorite places to go for a picnic. A lot of the trails that are up there were made by logging, pulling logs out of the hills. If I'm not mistaken, in the early days they used to be grazing up there. They'd be sheep from down below Riparia and down in there. They'd go up into the mountains and stay for the summer. I'm picking up again in the wintertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Moscow was a lot closer to the real countryside than it is today.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Remember, there was a road from here to Harvard over Moscow Mountain. I don't know if you've ever heard the name, Crumarine Gulch. That was the Crumarine Gulch Road. You could go right up over the top and down the other side. There was a sawmill over on the other side. I guess it was Princeton, not Harvard. It was a proposal by several Moscow men that had bought 40 or 80 acres up there on the big mountain. They proposed that we develop that Crumarine Quick Road. Up at the top, you'd turn to the right and go up to the big mountain and develop a park up there. Emmy Lewis was the instigator of that. He was a real estate man here in Moscow. He was a state senator and all that. He wanted to make it a city crowd. There was a county road back at the mountains that wound up over here in the Viola Grade. He had big plans of it being quite a tourist attraction. Abe Golf will land up there. Harry Powell owned a leaf burner on property up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was just in the higher reaches of Moscow Mountain. It was up the top there, the highest part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You couldn't get state funds for the road for some reason or other.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think the thing fell through because of financing, which a lot of us were sorry for because we thought that would have been a mighty good project. The Kiwanis Club was back at it. The Rotary Club was back at it. That it fell through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me the story about your scout, the one who you have that letter from, from St.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anthony's, the scout that he tried to reform?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was a scout with the pro -grade judge, Adrian Nelson. He was rather sympathetic for boys. He hated to send boys to the reform school. He came to me and says, I've got this boy. He's an illegitimate boy living with his grandmother.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He's in difficulty. He'd been writing checks on her and buying stuff that he shouldn't, especially candies and things like that. He asked me if I would like to take him over, see what we could do with him. I proposed the name to the scout group and asked them if they would want to carry on a humanitarian project and see what we could do with him. Well, there were two or three of the older scouts thought it would be a good thing. So we took him along and all he was aggressive. He was just going to learn everything in scouting. He just did good turns and all that and looked like we were making a lot of headway.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: In fact, he was instrumental in this flagpole that I showed you the picture of here. He went out in the mountains and got that flagpole and peeled it and brought it into the church.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We raced it there up on the church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was the Presbyterian church?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, years afterwards, after he was a colonel.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: After he was through scouting, he got into difficulty and he was sent again to reform school.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I guess he was there for a couple of years and they decided to pearl him out. He was on his way up here to Moscow and he and another pearl leave. They stopped in Boise and they sold a car.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That put him back in the reform school again. I think he was there for another couple of years. They pearled him out and he came up here. He went to work for the Potlatch Lumber Company in what capacity I don't know. He came in one day to the store after he had been up there a while and told me how much scouting taught him in the use of knots. In his work up there, he needed the knowledge of knots. It wasn't enough. I was glad he was doing so well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About an hour afterwards, Judge Nelson came through the store and I told him, you know, who do you suppose I saw?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Dave Cott came in here and told me how well he was doing up at Potlatch. He said, if you want to see him again, he's up in the county jail right now. I've forgotten what he did to get in. I only heard about him years and years later, but he still came back up into this country and he's around here now. I would judge he's a boy of another man now of probably 60 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The letter that he wrote to you from St. Anthony's was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did I get that?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You had that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The letter was very interesting because he was looking at you as a good friend and somebody had really helped him. He felt you really had helped him in scouting him. Oh, I'm sure, but he was too weak a character to stick with it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He's the only scout in all the scouting that I've gone through that's ever been sent to reform school, which is quite a record. While he's not the only boy we had difficulty with, this boy was never sent to reform school. I partly reformed him myself. He was a boy that sold spokesmen reviews and magazines on the street. I don't know if I told you this or not, but while he was on the scout troop, we had a program on where boys couldn't buy a uniform. We would try to raise through somebody a uniform so he could go to camp. Well, he approached the Moscow Hotel lobby. He says our scout troop is trying to raise money to get uniforms for some of the boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you like to contribute for it?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, this fellow told him he happened to be the random hotel barber shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He told him, he says, well, who's your scout master?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he told him Harry Sampson. He says, well, I'll see him about it. See what they need. And the shop says, don't do that. He doesn't want to be screwed on. Well, this fellow that told me about it, and there were several things that piled up on him about the same time, he would sell these magazines and tell him, says, I need an extra 50 cents or a dollar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you mind paying for your magazine a month in advance or your paper for a month in advance?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And he collected quite a bit by that, and then he didn't deliver in the magazine. So I took him, when I found that out, I found out from him who the people were that he hadn't delivered the magazine. And I took him to every one of those and made him pay it back.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, we didn't take him out of the troop or anything. We kept him on. And I remember years later,

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: we had William Gauss here in Moscow who was in the Air Corps, and he was ready for an advancement which required the FBI to investigate him. So they called on me to, because he gave me as a reference, to find out, or I sent in a reference about it, but they'd spelled the name wrong. This boy's name that we had trouble with was Quas. And Gauss, it's spelled the same way, it's G instead of a Q. Well, I made a mistake and made it G. So I couldn't recommend this boy for the position at all. And they couldn't understand it. So they sent a meth v. eye man over to the store to interview me, and he wouldn't talk to me in the store. He says, I've got my car out here. The curvie's just come out in the car. I want to talk to you. He showed me his bag of meth v. eye. So he showed me the recommendation or questionnaire that I had given. And when I saw him meeting him, I said, this isn't the boy that I had in the role of war for. And that kind of settled it. I said, well, this middle gal, he's the finest scout I ever had. I said, he's entitled to anything you can get. And that ended the interview.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This boy that you had had trouble with, this boy, he did go straighter after that?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He moved from here over to Seattle. His sister lived over in Seattle, and he went over to live with her. And I never heard about him since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to touch on a few more of these?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, Ollie, I'd like to mention a few of the backers of scouting and early scouting that were tremendous help in putting scouting over in Moscow. And I would put at the head of the list Homer David, Hermann Wilson, John Hecithorn, Harry Whittier, Abe Gulf, Reverend Sessions, G .L. Luke, Colonel Crispman, J .G. Eldridge, R .K. and Oscar Bonnet.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Henry Gauss, J .E. Rutherford, President Theophilus, H .H. Simpson, J .W. Barton, June Shoemaker, J .D. Davis, C .W. Hungerford, and I .C. Crawford. Those were all men that most of them were Kiwanis. And they did a tremendous job in backing the reorganization of scouting in 1926 and 7. It was through their help that I was able to organize five troops in Moscow. Got scout masters for all of them, got commitment for all of them, and merit badge examiners. So it was a real treat to have a backing of these men.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There's one little project that we did in Moscow. Moscow had street signs made on wooden boards stenciled. They'd put in years and years before. And long about 1927 or 8, a lot of those signs were G -Hull, a lot of them faded out. And we proposed to the city, if they would get us a stenchling set and the paint and the lumber, we would re -sign the streets in town. Well, they did that. And they wound up, they gave us $75 for the job because we did a good job of it. But this first or second scout troop that I had did the job. We did paint them white, stenciled them black, and then to preserve them we put a coat of shellac on top of that and put them all over town. Now those were the street sign Jews in Moscow until the Lions Club took the project on putting up the metal signs. And I think that has only been maybe 15 years ago, or yes about 15 years ago. So those served for a long time. There's one thing I want to ask you about and I don't expect you to say more than a bit about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you told me that 1925 scouting died and that's what it called a reorganization.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said this was because of an individual who was a scout master?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Scout executive.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We had a scout executive that was in the moral act that he did. And the financing had gotten in the bad shape too. And the whole thing together just fell apart. And there was no scouting in Moscow there for a year. And it was through the Kiwanis Club that I proposed that they wanted to get into Boys and Girls work. I would set up a scout program, which they did. And we were at that time under the Spokane Council.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Before street signs were put in, we performed a traffic survey of the main thoroughfare. And the scouts for one whole week had a traffic up on the corner of 3rd, corner of 6th, and the corner of Washington. They directed traffic and they also took the number of cars that passed that intersection in a 10 hour period.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: They also cited violations, cars that didn't pay attention to them and they took their license number down.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So I have the figures on the number of cars that passed the corner of 3rd and Main someplace. It's tremendous, the traffic even in those days. This was way back in 2008 or 2009.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there usually an officer directing traffic on the corner or was there usually no traffic?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No traffic cop, no. There was no traffic cop there at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the purpose of the survey? Was it to see what the needs were for traffic control?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It was to show the city the amount of traffic that was on the main thoroughfare.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I suppose to have stop signs and whatnot. But we chose the three most popular corners. If I'm not mistaken, as early as that, we'd have, it seems to me like there was 11, 12, 100 cars past the corner of 3rd and Main in 10 hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We're just about done. We've been waiting for those waffles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alright, you got them on?
